era_context: The early 1720s belong to the transition between the post-War of Spanish Succession settlement and the emerging Enlightenment. In Europe, Britain was consolidating parliamentary governance under Walpole, France was ending its Regency period, and Russia under Peter the Great was expanding along the Caspian. The Safavid Empire was in catastrophic collapse following the Afghan capture of Isfahan in 1722, with the Ottoman Empire and Russia both seizing Persian territories. In East Asia, the Qing dynasty's Yongzheng Emperor had just acceded and was centralizing power. The Tokugawa shogunate pursued the Kyōhō Reforms in Japan. In West Africa, the Ashanti and Dahomey kingdoms were expanding, while European colonial enterprises in the Americas continued to grow.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading zones and the Gold Coast
Interior South America beyond Iberian colonial administration records
Aboriginal Australia and interior Pacific Islands
Central Asia beyond Qing and Russian frontier records
Interior Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Laos, highland Burma)

events.title: J.S. Bach appointed Thomaskantor in Leipzig
events.region: Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Johann Sebastian Bach was appointed Thomaskantor and Director Musices in Leipzig on 5 May 1723, succeeding Johann Kuhnau. He assumed the role after leaving his position as Kapellmeister at Köthen, partly because the musical establishment there had diminished following Prince Leopold's marriage. The Leipzig post required Bach to compose cantatas for the principal churches, teach at the Thomasschule, and oversee the city's musical life. This appointment began the most prolific period of Bach's career, during which he composed the bulk of his cantata cycle, the St John Passion (premiered Good Friday 1724), the St Matthew Passion, the Mass in B minor, and other major works that would define the Western sacred music canon.
key_figures: Johann Sebastian Bach (composer, newly appointed Thomaskantor)
Johann Kuhnau (predecessor as Thomaskantor, d. 1722)
Georg Philipp Telemann (initially offered the post but declined)

sources.name: Leipzig town council minutes, May 1723
Christoph Wolff, Johann Sebastian Bach: The Learned Musician (2000)
Bach's letter to Georg Erdmann (28 October 1730), describing his move to Leipzig

events.certainty_note: Documented in Leipzig town council records and Bach's own correspondence.

events.title: Treaty of St. Petersburg between Russia and Persia
events.description: Signed on 12 September 1723, the Treaty of St. Petersburg formally ceded the Caspian coastal provinces of Shirvan, Gilan, Mazandaran, and Astarabad from Safavid Persia to Russia. The treaty was negotiated with Tahmasp II, the Safavid claimant who controlled little territory and desperately needed Russian support against the Afghan occupiers of Isfahan. Peter the Great had launched his Persian campaign in 1722 and occupied Derbent and Baku; the treaty ratified these conquests. Persia's simultaneous partition between Russia and the Ottoman Empire marked the effective end of Safavid sovereignty, though the ceded Caspian provinces would be returned to Persia by 1735 under Nader Shah.
key_figures: Peter I (Tsar of Russia)
Tahmasp II (Safavid claimant)
Ismail Beg (Persian envoy to St. Petersburg)

sources.name: Text of the Treaty of St. Petersburg (September 1723), in Russian state archives
Laurence Lockhart, The Fall of the Safavi Dynasty and the Afghan Occupation of Persia (1958)
Cambridge History of Iran, vol. 7 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in Russian archives; terms documented by multiple contemporary diplomatic sources.
cross_references: 1722-related events (fall of Isfahan, Peter's Persian campaign)

events.title: Ottoman Empire invades western Persia
events.description: Exploiting the collapse of Safavid authority following the Afghan capture of Isfahan in 1722, Ottoman forces under various commanders invaded western and northwestern Persia in 1723. Ottoman armies seized territories including parts of western Iran, Georgia, and Armenia. The Ottomans justified their intervention partly as protection of Sunni populations and partly as opportunistic expansion. This invasion proceeded in parallel with Russian advances on the Caspian coast, effectively partitioning the remnants of the Safavid state between two rival empires. Ottoman and Russian spheres of influence in Persia were later formally delineated in the Treaty of Constantinople (1724).
key_figures: Ahmed III (Ottoman Sultan)
Nevşehirli Damat İbrahim Pasha (Ottoman Grand Vizier)
Tahmasp II (Safavid claimant, attempting to resist)

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles (Raşid Mehmed Efendi, Tarih-i Raşid)
Lockhart, The Fall of the Safavi Dynasty (1958)
Rudi Matthee, Persia in Crisis: Safavid Decline and the Fall of Isfahan (2012)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Ottoman court chronicles and confirmed by multiple regional sources.

events.title: Mir Mahmud's increasingly tyrannical rule in Isfahan
events.description: Mir Mahmud Hotaki, the Afghan ruler who had captured Isfahan in October 1722, spent 1723 attempting to consolidate his control over central Persia. His rule grew increasingly brutal: he ordered massacres of Safavid princes and Persian notables, fearing conspiracies. His authority barely extended beyond Isfahan and its hinterland, as the rest of Persia fragmented among Safavid loyalists, Ottoman invaders, and Russian forces. Mahmud's mental state reportedly deteriorated over the course of the year. He would be overthrown and killed by his cousin Ashraf Hotaki in 1725.
key_figures: Mir Mahmud Hotaki (Afghan ruler of Isfahan)
Sultan Husayn (deposed Safavid shah, held captive in Isfahan)

sources.name: Krusinski, The History of the Late Revolutions of Persia (1728, based on firsthand observation)
Lockhart, The Fall of the Safavi Dynasty (1958)

events.certainty_note: Father Krusinski, a Jesuit resident in Isfahan, provided contemporary eyewitness accounts of Mahmud's rule.

events.title: Louis XV declared of age; end of French Regency
events.description: On 15 February 1723, King Louis XV of France, having reached the age of thirteen, was formally declared of age in a lit de justice ceremony at the Parlement of Paris, ending the Regency that had governed France since Louis XIV's death in 1715. Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, who had served as regent, was appointed chief minister and continued to wield effective power. When Orléans died suddenly on 2 December 1723, Louis XV appointed Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon, as the new chief minister. The transition marked the formal close of the Regency era, a period characterised by financial experimentation (John Law's system), diplomatic realignment, and cultural liberalisation.
key_figures: Louis XV (King of France, age 13)
Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (Regent, then chief minister; d. December 1723)
Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon (successor as chief minister)

sources.name: Registers of the Parlement of Paris, February 1723
Saint-Simon, Mémoires (covering the Regency period)
Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie, The Ancien Régime: A History of France 1610–1774 (1996)

events.certainty_note: Lit de justice ceremony and its date recorded in parliamentary registers and multiple memoirs.

events.title: Death of Philippe II, Duke of Orléans
events.description: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, died suddenly on 2 December 1723 at Versailles, likely of a stroke. He had governed France as regent from 1715 to February 1723 and then as chief minister. His regency was marked by the disastrous Mississippi Bubble, diplomatic reversals (the alliance with Britain), and a relaxation of the austere tone of late Louis XIV's court. His death opened a power vacuum that the Duke of Bourbon filled, beginning a brief and generally unsuccessful ministry that lasted until Cardinal Fleury's rise in 1726.
key_figures: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (former Regent, d. 2 December 1723)
Louis XV (King of France)
Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon (successor as chief minister)

sources.name: Saint-Simon, Mémoires
Court gazette and diplomatic correspondence, December 1723

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances well documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: The Black Act enacted in Britain
events.description: The Waltham Black Act (9 Geo. I c. 22), passed by Parliament in May 1723, created over fifty new capital offences related to poaching, smuggling, and property destruction, particularly targeting armed or disguised persons found in forests, parks, or warrens. The Act was prompted by the activities of the 'Waltham Blacks,' bands of poachers in the royal forests of Hampshire and Berkshire who blackened their faces to avoid identification. E.P. Thompson's influential study Whigs and Hunters (1975) argued the Act represented a watershed in the use of criminal law to defend aristocratic property rights, though subsequent historians have debated whether it was exceptional or part of broader trends in English criminal legislation.
key_figures: Robert Walpole (Prime Minister / First Lord of the Treasury)
Charles Townshend, 2nd Viscount Townshend (Secretary of State)

sources.name: 9 Geo. I c. 22 (statute text, 1723)
E.P. Thompson, Whigs and Hunters: The Origin of the Black Act (1975)
John Broad, 'Whigs and Deer-Stealers in Other Guises,' Past & Present 119 (1988)

events.certainty_note: Statute text survives; parliamentary debates and Home Office correspondence document the context.

events.title: Exile of Bishop Atterbury from Britain
events.description: Francis Atterbury, Bishop of Rochester, was banished from Britain by Act of Parliament in June 1723 following his conviction for involvement in the Atterbury Plot, a Jacobite conspiracy to restore the Stuart pretender James III. The plot, uncovered in 1722, had envisioned a coordinated rising with French support. Atterbury's trial before the House of Lords was a major political event; Walpole used it to discredit the Tory opposition and consolidate Whig dominance. Atterbury went into exile in France, where he remained until his death in 1732. The affair reinforced the political marginalisation of Jacobitism in mainstream British politics.
key_figures: Francis Atterbury (Bishop of Rochester, Jacobite conspirator)
Robert Walpole (Prime Minister, chief prosecutor of the plot)
James Francis Edward Stuart ('James III,' the Old Pretender)

sources.name: Journals of the House of Lords, 1722–1723 (trial proceedings)
Eveline Cruickshanks, The Atterbury Plot (2004)
G.V. Bennett, The Tory Crisis in Church and State (1975)

events.certainty_note: Trial proceedings and parliamentary records survive in detail.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor consolidates Qing power
events.description: The Yongzheng Emperor (Yinzhen), who ascended the Qing throne in December 1722, spent 1723 consolidating his authority against rival princes and reforming the administration. He launched a vigorous anti-corruption campaign, investigated the finances of his brothers (particularly Yinsi and Yintang, who had contested the succession), and began implementing fiscal reforms including the amalgamation of the head tax into the land tax (tan ding ru mu). He also moved to restrict Christianity, issuing edicts that curtailed missionary activity and closed many churches outside of Beijing, reversing the relative tolerance of the Kangxi era.
key_figures: Yongzheng Emperor (Aisin Gioro Yinzhen, Qing Emperor r. 1722–1735)
Nian Gengyao (military commander, initially trusted by Yongzheng)
Longkodo (Grand Secretary, initially a key supporter)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Yongzheng reign
Jonathan Spence, The Search for Modern China (1990)
Madeleine Zelin, The Magistrate's Tael: Rationalizing Fiscal Reform in Eighteenth-Century Ch'ing China (1984)

events.certainty_note: Qing court records (Shilu, palace memorials) provide detailed contemporary documentation of Yongzheng's early reign.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor restricts Christianity in China
events.description: In 1723 the Yongzheng Emperor issued edicts restricting Christian missionary activity in China, ordering the closure of churches in the provinces and the concentration of missionaries in Beijing and Guangzhou. This reversed the relatively tolerant approach of his father, the Kangxi Emperor, and was influenced by the ongoing Chinese Rites Controversy (a dispute between the papacy and the Jesuits over whether Chinese converts could continue ancestral rites). The restrictions drove Christianity underground in many provinces and significantly limited the growth of Catholicism in China for the remainder of the Qing period.
key_figures: Yongzheng Emperor (Qing Emperor)
Fujian provincial officials (who reported on Christian communities)

sources.name: Qing Shilu, Yongzheng reign, edicts on heterodox sects
Lettres édifiantes et curieuses (Jesuit missionary correspondence)
D.E. Mungello, The Chinese Rites Controversy: Its History and Meaning (1994)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Qing court records and Jesuit missionary correspondence.

events.title: Tokugawa Yoshimune's Kyōhō Reforms continue in Japan
events.description: Shogun Tokugawa Yoshimune continued his Kyōhō Reforms (initiated c. 1716) through 1723, addressing the shogunate's chronic fiscal crisis. Measures included austerity mandates for samurai households, encouragement of new rice-paddy development, and adjustments to the rice-based tax system. Yoshimune also promoted the importation of Western scientific books (excluding those on Christianity), partially relaxing the ban on foreign literature that had been in place since the early seclusion edicts. These reforms stabilised shogunal finances in the short term and represented one of the most significant reform efforts of the Edo period.
key_figures: Tokugawa Yoshimune (Shogun, r. 1716–1745)
Ooka Tadasuke (town magistrate of Edo, associated with reform implementation)

sources.name: Tokugawa Jikki (official chronicle of the Tokugawa shogunate)
Harold Bolitho, 'The Tempō Crisis,' in Cambridge History of Japan, vol. 4 (1991) (covers Kyōhō reforms comparatively)
Tsuji Tatsuya, 'Politics in the Eighteenth Century,' in Cambridge History of Japan, vol. 4 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Shogunal records and bakufu administrative documents provide contemporary evidence of reform measures.

events.title: Peter the Great founds Yekaterinburg
events.description: The city of Yekaterinburg was founded on 7 November 1723 (OS) as an ironworks settlement on the Iset River in the Ural Mountains, named after Empress Catherine I. Established under the direction of Vasily Tatishchev and Georg Wilhelm de Gennin, the settlement centred on a new iron and copper smelting works that became one of the largest metallurgical plants in Russia. The founding was part of Peter the Great's broader programme to develop Russian industry, particularly metals production, in the Urals. Yekaterinburg would grow into the major industrial and administrative centre of the Urals region.
key_figures: Peter I (Tsar of Russia)
Vasily Tatishchev (mining administrator and historian)
Georg Wilhelm de Gennin (military engineer and factory director)

sources.name: De Gennin's reports to the Berg-Collegium (mining administration), 1723
Lindsey Hughes, Russia in the Age of Peter the Great (1998)

events.certainty_note: Foundation date documented in administrative records and de Gennin's correspondence.

events.title: Dahomey kingdom expands under King Agaja
events.description: King Agaja of Dahomey continued the kingdom's military expansion in the early 1720s, building toward his conquest of the coastal kingdom of Allada in 1724. By 1723, Dahomey was already exerting military pressure on its southern neighbours and positioning itself to gain direct access to the Atlantic slave trade, which had previously been mediated through coastal middlemen kingdoms. Agaja's military reforms, including the development of a standing army and (according to some traditions) the incorporation of female soldiers, were transforming Dahomey from an inland polity into a significant regional power.
key_figures: Agaja (King of Dahomey, r. c. 1718–1740)

sources.name: European trading company records from Ouidah (Dutch and English factors)
Robin Law, The Slave Coast of West Africa 1550–1750 (1991)
I.A. Akinjogbin, Dahomey and Its Neighbours 1708–1818 (1967)

events.certainty_note: Precise chronology of Dahomey's pre-Allada campaigns is uncertain; European coastal records provide indirect evidence of interior military activity.

events.title: Birth of Adam Smith
events.description: Adam Smith was baptised on 5 June 1723 in Kirkcaldy, Fife, Scotland (his exact birth date is unrecorded but was shortly before). Smith would become the most influential political economist in history, authoring The Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759) and An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations (1776). While his significance is retrospective rather than contemporary to 1723, his birth is noted here for its outsized subsequent impact on economic thought, policy, and the intellectual foundations of modern capitalism.
key_figures: Adam Smith (future economist and philosopher)
Margaret Douglas (his mother)

sources.name: Kirkcaldy parish baptismal register, 5 June 1723
Ian Simpson Ross, The Life of Adam Smith (1995; 2nd ed. 2010)

events.certainty_note: Baptismal date confirmed in parish records. Exact birth date unknown but within days of baptism.

events.title: Death of Sir Christopher Wren
events.description: Sir Christopher Wren, the architect responsible for rebuilding much of London after the Great Fire of 1666, died on 25 February 1723 at the age of ninety. His greatest achievement was St Paul's Cathedral, completed in 1710, along with over fifty other London churches. Wren also served as Surveyor of the King's Works for nearly fifty years and made significant contributions to mathematics and astronomy as a founding member of the Royal Society. He was buried in St Paul's Cathedral, where the epitaph on his tomb reads: 'Lector, si monumentum requiris, circumspice' (Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you).
key_figures: Sir Christopher Wren (architect, d. 25 February 1723)

sources.name: Parentalia, or Memoirs of the Family of the Wrens (compiled by his son, published 1750)
Adrian Tinniswood, His Invention So Fertile: A Life of Christopher Wren (2001)
Contemporary obituary notices in London newspapers, February–March 1723

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances of death well documented in contemporary records.

events.title: Nizam-ul-Mulk consolidates autonomy in Hyderabad
events.description: Qamar-ud-din Khan, later titled Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I, continued consolidating his effective autonomy in the Deccan during 1723, having been appointed governor of the Deccan provinces by Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah. Though nominally a Mughal viceroy, the Nizam increasingly operated as an independent ruler, resisting attempts by the Mughal court and the Sayyid brothers' successors to control him. His establishment of a de facto independent state based at Hyderabad was a key step in the Mughal Empire's fragmentation into successor states, a process that would define eighteenth-century South Asian politics.
key_figures: Qamar-ud-din Khan / Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (Mughal governor of the Deccan)
Muhammad Shah (Mughal Emperor, r. 1719–1748)

sources.name: Mughal court chronicles and farmans (imperial orders)
Yusuf Husain Khan, The First Nizam (1963)
Muzaffar Alam, The Crisis of Empire in Mughal North India (1986)

events.certainty_note: Mughal administrative records and the Nizam's own correspondence document the political dynamics.

events.title: Prussia establishes General Directory
events.description: King Frederick William I of Prussia created the General-Ober-Finanz-Kriegs-und-Domänen-Direktorium (General Directory) on 20 January 1723, merging the General War Commissariat and the General Finance Directory into a single supreme administrative body. This centralising reform was a cornerstone of Prussian state-building, creating a unified bureaucracy responsible for military finance, taxation, economic policy, and domain management. The General Directory became the institutional backbone of Prussian governance and a model for centralised absolutist administration that would persist until the Stein-Hardenberg reforms of 1808.
key_figures: Frederick William I (King of Prussia, the 'Soldier King')

sources.name: Royal edict establishing the General Directory, 20 January 1723
Hans Rosenberg, Bureaucracy, Aristocracy and Autocracy: The Prussian Experience 1660–1815 (1958)
Christopher Clark, Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia 1600–1947 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Founding edict survives; institutional history well documented in Prussian state archives.

events.title: Wood's Halfpence controversy intensifies in Ireland
events.description: The controversy over Wood's Halfpence — a patent granted to the English manufacturer William Wood to mint copper coinage for Ireland — intensified in 1723 as Irish opposition mobilised. The Irish Parliament and public objected that the patent had been granted without Irish consultation, that the coinage would flood Ireland with debased currency, and that the profits flowed to Wood and his English backers (including the King's mistress, the Duchess of Kendal, who had sold the patent). Jonathan Swift would soon enter the controversy with his Drapier's Letters (1724). The affair became a significant flashpoint in Anglo-Irish constitutional tensions.
key_figures: William Wood (English ironmaster, patent holder)
Duchess of Kendal (Melusine von der Schulenburg, who brokered the patent)
Jonathan Swift (Dean of St Patrick's, entered the controversy in 1724)

sources.name: Irish House of Commons journals and addresses, 1723
Patrick Kelly, 'The Irish Woollen Export Prohibition Act of 1699: Kearney Revisited,' Irish Economic and Social History 7 (1980)
James Woolley, 'Swift's Drapier's Letters,' in The Cambridge Companion to Jonathan Swift (2003)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary records, pamphlet literature, and Swift's subsequent writings document the controversy in detail.

events.title: Peter the Great's Table of Ranks reinforced
events.description: Peter the Great continued to implement and enforce the Table of Ranks (Tabel' o rangakh), promulgated in January 1722, which reorganised Russian state service by ranking all military, civil, and court positions into fourteen grades. By 1723, the system was actively reshaping Russian elite society: advancement depended on state service rather than birth alone, and commoners who reached the eighth rank automatically gained hereditary nobility. While the old boyar aristocracy resisted, the Table of Ranks created the institutional framework for Russian bureaucratic governance that persisted until 1917.
key_figures: Peter I (Tsar of Russia)

sources.name: Tabel' o rangakh (Table of Ranks), promulgated 24 January 1722
Brenda Meehan-Waters, Autocracy and Aristocracy: The Russian Service Elite of 1730 (1982)

events.certainty_note: The Table of Ranks is a well-documented legislative act; its implementation in 1722–23 is confirmed in administrative records.

events.title: Vietnamese Lê-Trịnh administration in Đàng Ngoài
events.description: In Đàng Ngoài (northern Vietnam), the Trịnh lords continued to exercise effective power while maintaining the fiction of Lê dynasty sovereignty. The period was one of relative stability following earlier civil conflicts, with the Trịnh administration managing fiscal and military affairs. The southern Nguyễn lords controlled Đàng Trong (central and southern Vietnam) as a separate polity. Documentation for this specific year is limited in Western-language sources, though Vietnamese court annals (Đại Việt Sử Ký Toàn Thư and continuations) record administrative activities.
key_figures: Trịnh Cương (Trịnh lord, r. 1709–1729)
Lê Dụ Tông (Lê Emperor, r. 1705–1729, nominal ruler)

sources.name: Đại Việt Sử Ký Tục Biên (Continued Annals of Đại Việt)
K.W. Taylor, A History of the Vietnamese (2013)

events.certainty_note: Vietnamese court annals exist for this period but detailed year-by-year events are less accessible in Western scholarship. General political framework is well established.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly cited claims about this period merit qualification. (1) The Yongzheng Emperor's anti-Christian edicts are sometimes framed as a sharp break from Kangxi-era tolerance, but recent scholarship (e.g., Mungello, Standaert) notes that Kangxi himself had already restricted certain missionary activities in his final years, and provincial enforcement of anti-Christian measures was uneven under both emperors — the 1723 edicts represent an escalation, not an unprecedented reversal. (2) The Treaty of St. Petersburg is sometimes presented as a significant Russian territorial gain, but the ceded Caspian provinces proved extremely difficult and costly for Russia to hold; Peter's successors progressively withdrew, and all territories were returned by the Treaty of Rasht (1732) and Treaty of Ganja (1735). The 'conquest' was largely ephemeral. (3) Frederick William I's General Directory is often cited as a model of Prussian efficiency, but Rosenberg and others note that jurisdictional conflicts between its departments persisted and that the king frequently bypassed his own bureaucracy through personal intervention. (4) The Black Act's significance as argued by E.P. Thompson has been partially challenged by historians such as John Broad and Peter King, who argue Thompson overestimated its novelty and underestimated the role of local interests (rather than class warfare) in its passage.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1723 is rich for Europe, Russia, and Qing China, supported by extensive state archives, parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, and court chronicles. The Safavid collapse is unusually well documented thanks to European observers (Krusinski, trading company agents) resident in Persia. For South and Southeast Asia, the record depends heavily on court chronicles that may underrepresent dissent and non-elite perspectives. West African events are primarily known through European coastal trading records, which provide an outsider's and often distorted view of interior political dynamics. The Americas are documented mainly through colonial administrative records, which centre European interests. The surviving sources for this year overwhelmingly reflect the perspectives of state elites and literate classes.

graph_edges.note: Russian-Persian treaty was a direct consequence of the Afghan capture of Isfahan in 1722 and the collapse of Safavid authority
Ottoman invasion exploited the power vacuum created by the Afghan conquest of Isfahan
Russian-Persian treaty prompted Ottoman-Russian negotiations to partition Persian territories, formalised in 1724
Louis XV's majority ended the formal Regency; Orléans' death ended the Regency era's political influence
Orléans' death began the chain of ministerial succession leading to Cardinal Fleury's rise in 1726
Both the Black Act and Atterbury exile reflect Walpole's consolidation of Whig authority in 1723
Restriction of Christianity was part of Yongzheng's broader programme of political and ideological consolidation
Both Prussian General Directory and Yekaterinburg founding reflect parallel European state-building and administrative centralisation
The 1723 Irish opposition to Wood's Halfpence directly prompted Swift's Drapier's Letters in 1724
Nizam-ul-Mulk's autonomy in Hyderabad was a major episode in the broader fragmentation of the Mughal Empire
Dahomey's military preparations in 1723 preceded the conquest of Allada in 1724
